title: Driving Test

jokes.text: Johnny asks David if he has his wallet, then pivots immediately to asking if he has his license — already telegraphing something is wrong before David even answers.
jokes.setup: Johnny asks 'You got your wallet?' as a reasonable pre-drive checklist item, implying normal parental caution.
jokes.punchline: He pivots immediately to 'And what about your license?' — revealing the real concern and setting up the cascade of bad news.
jokes.explanation: A functional opener that efficiently sets up the episode's A-plot. The wallet-to-license pivot is a small misdirect that works, but it's largely mechanical scene-setting rather than a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do you have your license?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's fine.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I didn't ask if it was fine, I asked if you have it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, and I'm telling you it's fine.

jokes.text: David: '(Sighs) It's expired.'
jokes.setup: Johnny demands David go get his license before driving.
jokes.punchline: The long sigh before the admission, followed by the flat 'it's expired' — the sigh doing all the work of signaling David knew this was coming and hoped to avoid it.
jokes.explanation: Three words and a sigh delivering maximum information — that David knew, tried to hide it, and hoped to skate by. The economy here is genuinely impressive. The comedic pause built into the sigh is a performance beat that rewards rewatches.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, you guys, David failed his driving test. Again. He parallel parked into a storefront. A storefront!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And when the examiner told him he failed, he cried. Like, actually cried. It was so embarrassing.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'When did your license expire?!' / David: 'I don't know! Like, last month.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is outraged that David's license is expired.
jokes.punchline: 'I don't know!' followed immediately by the very specific 'Like, last month' — contradicting his own claim of ignorance instantly.
jokes.explanation: The self-contradiction within a single beat is a classic David move and it's well-executed here. He's too smart to genuinely not know, but too defensive to admit knowledge up front. The 'like' softening 'last month' is perfect vocal character work.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: *gasps dramatically*

jokes.text: David: 'Well, I drove the car yesterday!' / Johnny: 'Well, that's illegal, David!'
jokes.setup: Johnny says David can't drive because his license is expired.
jokes.punchline: David's defense is that he already committed the crime yesterday — using the precedent of already doing the illegal thing as an argument for why it's okay to continue.
jokes.explanation: David's broken logic ('I already did it, so it's fine') is a great character beat that feels true to someone raised without consequences. Johnny's flat 'That's illegal, David' lands well as a completely reasonable response to a completely unreasonable argument.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You failed your driving test four times.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I didn't fail. I was asked to leave.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's failing.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's not the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You were banned from two DMVs.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Those were politically motivated.

jokes.text: Alexis gasps dramatically and announces David took 16 tries to get his license.
jokes.setup: Alexis arrives and asks to use the car, then learns David's license is expired.
jokes.punchline: Her theatrical gasp followed by '16 tries' — an immediately escalating revelation that reframes David's driving situation as catastrophically worse than we knew.
jokes.explanation: Alexis's gasped, performative concern masking genuine delight in her brother's failings is perfectly in-character. '16 tries' is a big number that lands well, especially because David immediately begins correcting it, which itself becomes a joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It didn't take 16 tries.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh really? Because I have a list here. Try number one...

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: When you tried to fix the sink and flooded the entire back room.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Try number two: the time you thought you could rewire the electrical panel.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Try number three, four, five, six...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, I get it.

jokes.text: David's meticulous correction of the 16 tries claim: 'I failed the written test twice, and then took the driving test, and passed! And then your learner's permit expired, and you had to do the written test again, which you... failed.'
jokes.setup: David insists he did NOT take 16 tries and begins carefully itemizing his actual testing history to prove Alexis wrong.
jokes.punchline: His own detailed correction keeps revealing more failures — the very act of defending himself makes it worse, culminating in Alexis completing the story: 'and you failed the practical two more times.'
jokes.explanation: This is beautifully structured escalation comedy. The joke is that David's defense becomes an indictment — every item he names to refute '16 tries' confirms a fresh failure, and Alexis cheerfully completes the tally. The structure earns a high score because it builds across multiple beats, each worse than the last. Character-specific to the Rose sibling dynamic where Alexis weaponizes cheerfulness.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, darling, I've consulted with several medical professionals, and they've all come to the same conclusion. You are suffering from a severe case of incompetence-itis. It's a degenerative condition, I'm afraid, with no known cure.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Oh, the poor thing has crippling test anxiety. We all know that.' — delivered as if this is well-documented family lore while immediately asking for the car keys.
jokes.setup: The family is in a heated argument about David's repeated test failures.
jokes.punchline: Moira breezes in, acknowledges David's 'crippling test anxiety' as established fact, and immediately pivots to asking for the keys — treating her son's suffering as a minor footnote before her own needs.
jokes.explanation: This is quintessential Moira — she validates with theatrical empathy ('the poor thing') and then demonstrates zero actual empathy by pivoting to her own agenda in the same breath. 'We all know that' is a great line because it implies years of everyone tolerating David's anxiety as a documented family condition. The character integration is near-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: David, darling, would you drive me to the store?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Moira, we just spent the last twenty minutes talking about how David can't drive.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that was before I needed to go to the store.

jokes.text: Moira asks David to 'drive your mummy to Town Hall' immediately after it's been established he cannot legally drive.
jokes.setup: The entire preceding scene has established David's license is expired and he cannot drive.
jokes.punchline: Moira, who was present for the entire conversation, immediately asks David to drive her — as if none of it applied.
jokes.explanation: A solid Moira beat — her selective processing of information that doesn't serve her is a reliable character engine. The joke works but is slightly undercut by the scene immediately correcting her, reducing its stand-alone impact.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Did you take my wallet?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I don't know what you're talking about.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Okay, I will drive.' / Johnny: 'You got your wallet?' / Alexis: 'It's fine.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asked David 'You got your wallet?' at the top of the scene, and David's 'It's fine' deflection revealed his license was expired.
jokes.punchline: The exact same exchange repeats with Alexis, implying her license situation may be similarly 'fine' — the callback landing before we even need the confirmation.
jokes.explanation: This is among the best jokes in the episode — an exquisitely timed callback that requires zero additional dialogue to land. Alexis's 'It's fine' is a perfect echo of David's, and the audience already knows exactly what 'it's fine' means in this context. The economy is exceptional: two words do the work of an entire scene. Structure score is high because the setup was planted organically two minutes earlier and the callback arrives at precisely the right moment. This rewards the attentive viewer.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I would honestly rather contract a fatal disease than go through all of this paperwork.

jokes.text: David: 'I'm just hoping I contract some deadly lung infection from the dust before I have to open another one.'
jokes.setup: David is surrounded by boxes of business paperwork he has to sort through at the store.
jokes.punchline: His preferred alternative to doing paperwork is dying of a lung infection — delivered as a casual, practical preference.
jokes.explanation: A good David line — the dark hyperbole is proportionate to his dramatic personality and the mundane trigger (paperwork) makes the death-wish funnier. The casual delivery implied by the dialogue structure ('I'm just hoping') elevates it above standard complaining.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, I've been going through all these files and I'm realizing we haven't filed our corporate taxes in three years. Three years!

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, I know.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You know? How do you know that so casually?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, I also haven't filed my personal taxes in five years, so...

jokes.text: Patrick: 'Um well, I wouldn't really worry about it, I managed several galleries, and never once looked at the paperwork, so. It either all comes together, or it doesn't. Ultimately.' / David: 'Did it ever come together?'
jokes.setup: Patrick tries to reassure David about the overwhelming paperwork by citing his own gallery management experience and breezy philosophy.
jokes.punchline: David's flat 'Did it ever come together?' — puncturing Patrick's confidence with the obvious follow-up question that reveals the advice was useless.
jokes.explanation: David's four-word reply is perfectly calibrated — just pointed enough to deflate Patrick without being cruel. The humor comes from Patrick's non-answer ('Um, I think we're getting sidetracked') confirming the galleries did not, in fact, come together. The structure is clean: breezy confidence → piercing question → evasion.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Did your neglect ever actually work out?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I think we're getting sidetracked.

jokes.text: Patrick's roundabout build-up to asking David for a ride to his driving test: 'What I find helpful when I get overwhelmed is actually taking a step away for like, 15 minutes to an hour, to um, to maybe two hours, and ah, helping... people.'
jokes.setup: Patrick seems to be offering genuine productivity advice about taking breaks.
jokes.punchline: The 'helping... people' trails off awkwardly, revealing it was never advice — it was a preamble to a very specific ask, as David immediately calls out: 'I feel like there's a request coming on.'
jokes.explanation: The escalating time estimate (15 minutes → an hour → maybe two hours) is a nice comic rhythm, and the trailing 'helping... people' is a gentle misdirect. David's pre-empting call-out ('I feel like there's a request coming on') adds a nice layer of sitcom-awareness.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I prefer to think of it as owning my failures. And you're the bad employee.

jokes.text: Patrick: 'I am looking for a very lucky person to drive me to my driver's test.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has been obliquely building up to asking a favor.
jokes.punchline: Framing the person who has to drive him to a driving test as 'very lucky' — delivered with complete sincerity as if this is a genuine opportunity.
jokes.explanation: Mild irony that works because of the understated delivery implied by the script. The contrast between 'very lucky' and the mundane task of sitting in a car while someone takes a test is the joke. Solid but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: You know, when things get overwhelming, I find it helpful to take a short break. Maybe step away for five minutes, clear your head, get some fresh air.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Actually, make that ten minutes. Ten minutes is really the optimal time to reset mentally.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Or, you know, fifteen. Fifteen minutes allows for a proper walk, maybe grab a coffee.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: A half hour, really. A solid thirty minutes is when you start to see real benefits.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Maybe take the rest of the day. I mean, why fight it?

jokes.text: David: 'Oh yeah, that's not gonna happen right now.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has just made his elaborate case for David to drive him to his driving test.
jokes.punchline: David's flat, immediate refusal — no softening, no apology, just clean rejection.
jokes.explanation: Economically delivered and true to David's bluntness, but the joke is primarily functional — it closes Patrick's request and transitions scenes. Not much craft beyond economy of delivery.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Patrick, you can't just ask someone to be your lucky person. That's not a thing.

jokes.text: Patrick's follow-up: 'I would love to run some errands, or you know, maybe pick something up, I just-I just need a valid driver's license.'
jokes.setup: Patrick has been rejected and seems to be pivoting to other ways he could help David.
jokes.punchline: The pivot reveals he's not actually offering to help — he's still maneuvering toward getting a ride, this time by framing his test as the thing standing between David and his errand-running. The 'I just need a valid driver's license' is the tell.
jokes.explanation: Patrick's persistent maneuvering is charming, but this beat is slightly redundant — we've already clocked his scheme. Still, 'I just need a valid driver's license' is a good, plain-spoken punchline.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: I'd be happy to help you with your errands.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Really? That would be amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Well, actually, I can't do your errands because I need you to drive me to the car rental place first.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So you need me to drive you to rent a car so you can do errands for me?

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Moira: 'I don't know why you didn't ask me first, David. I have my license in seven different countries, and I have my F Class.'
jokes.setup: David apparently didn't ask Moira to take him to his driving test.
jokes.punchline: Moira has a license in 'seven different countries' and an F Class (for transport trucks) — both absurdly impressive and completely unexplained, delivered as if these are self-evidently relevant credentials.
jokes.explanation: This is a great Moira line — the specificity of 'seven different countries' suggests a chaotic international life never fully explained, and the F Class license for transport trucks is an absurd detail that demands and resists explanation simultaneously. 'I had a lot of people to move!' is a perfect non-explanation. Very high character integration because this could only be Moira.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wow, Patrick. What an incredible breakthrough. You've finally figured out that showing up to someone's event uninvited and unannounced might not be the best timing. Next, you'll discover that water is wet.

jokes.text: David: 'Isn't that for transport trucks?!' / Moira: 'Yes, well, I had a lot of people to move!'
jokes.setup: Moira mentions she has an F Class license.
jokes.punchline: David's incredulous question is answered with 'I had a lot of people to move!' — a complete non-explanation that implies a rich, mysterious, and somewhat alarming backstory.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'I had a lot of people to move' is Moira at her peak — a completely insufficient explanation delivered with total certainty, implying a past that is both glamorous and vaguely criminal. The economy is near-perfect: five words open up an entire mystery while closing the question. High quotability because it's specific, character-driven, and lands as a standalone. This approaches the upper tier of the scoring range.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I have licenses in seven countries.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And wear your best hair perfume.'
jokes.setup: Moira is giving advice about passing a driving test.
jokes.punchline: 'Hair perfume' — not just perfume, but a specific category of fragrance applied to hair — delivered as a serious tactical suggestion for test-taking success.
jokes.explanation: Two words, maximum Moira. 'Hair perfume' as a specific beauty product category is an absurd but plausible Moira detail, and deploying it as a driving test strategy reveals her entire worldview: charm and presentation are the solution to all problems. Excellent economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: She has a commercial trucking license because she 'had a lot of people to move'.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Okay, you try parallel parking in a burka, David. No amount of flirting can get you out of that, trust me!'
jokes.setup: David accuses Moira of flirting her way through driving tests.
jokes.punchline: Moira confirms she does flirt during driving tests but invokes the specific exception of parallel parking while wearing a burka — a revelation that implies she has taken a driving test while wearing a burka, which raises entirely new questions.
jokes.explanation: An exceptional Moira line. Rather than denying the flirting, she confirms it and provides a caveat — which is funnier than a denial would be. The specificity of 'parallel parking in a burka' implies she has extensive international driving test experience that includes this exact scenario. The 'trust me' seals it — she speaks from experience. This is a line that gets better as you think about what it implies. High quotability and strong rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Not everyone can flirt their way to a license.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, you try parallel parking in a bandage dress.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Oh, that reminds me, can you hold the wheel for a second, I need to check my phone.'
jokes.setup: Alexis has been advising David about not worrying and relaxing while they're driving.
jokes.punchline: Mid-sentence, mid-driving, Alexis casually asks the anxious passenger to take the wheel so she can check her phone — demonstrating total indifference to danger.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed character comedy — Alexis has just been telling David to relax and not worry, and she immediately creates a terrifying situation. The juxtaposition between her advice and her action is the joke. Character-specific because Alexis's blithe disregard for consequences is a core trait.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, I'm getting a text from the group chat.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, eyes on the road!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I am looking at the road. I'm also looking at my phone. I'm very talented.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That is not talent, that is a felony!

jokes.text: David: 'Oh my God! I'll tell you what's not helping me right now! This!'
jokes.setup: Alexis has asked David to hold the steering wheel while she checks her phone.
jokes.punchline: David's outburst of pure panic — 'This!' being the most efficient possible summary of everything wrong with the situation.
jokes.explanation: A solid panic reaction — 'This!' as the punchline word is economically perfect. But the joke is primarily reactive and relies on the visual of the situation rather than the line itself.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What? I've had a child drive me through Manhattan.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, it was fine.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Hey, relax, David. I once had a 7-year-old drive me around Mumbai, so I'm pretty sure you're cool to steer for a sec.'
jokes.setup: David is panicking about holding the steering wheel while Alexis checks her phone.
jokes.punchline: Alexis's reassurance is that she's experienced worse — a 7-year-old chauffeur in Mumbai — and that this is therefore fine. The logic is unassailable and horrifying.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'I once had a 7-year-old drive me around Mumbai' is a perfect Alexis Rose detail — specific, absurd, implying a lawless international life, and offered as a comfort. The comparative logic ('you're cool to steer for a sec') is faultless within Alexis's worldview. Extremely quotable, high character integration, and the image it conjures rewards thinking about. This is comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor example.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's just a minor plumbing issue. We'll get it fixed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, darling. Hardly catastrophic.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: A minor plumbing issue? MINOR?! Do you have any idea what's happening in my life right now?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm falling apart. Everything is falling apart.

jokes.text: Moira enters the motel office and immediately launches into complaints about the leaky faucet being audible from 'our bathroom' — while it's technically in David and Alexis's bathroom.
jokes.setup: Moira tells Stevie there's a leaky faucet in 'David and Alexis' bathroom' and says she can hear it 'clear as day.'
jokes.punchline: Stevie notes 'But it's not [in your bathroom]' — exposing that Moira is complaining about her children's inconvenience as if it were her own, which everyone sees through immediately.
jokes.explanation: A functional Moira bit — her inability to distinguish between her inconvenience and others' is on-brand — but the comedic payoff is mild since Stevie's flat correction does most of the work. The scene is more setup for the emotional beat that follows.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm having some serious business problems.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, a man?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: No, it's not about a man.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A woman?

jokes.text: Stevie suddenly bursts into tears in the middle of what seemed like a normal motel management conversation, as Moira is mid-complaint about the faucet.
jokes.setup: Moira is dramatically listing the sounds of the dripping faucet ('the incessant tap, tap, tap') as if presenting evidence to a jury.
jokes.punchline: Stevie begins sobbing — not about the faucet, but about her overwhelming anxiety about running the motel — creating an abrupt tonal shift that Moira and Johnny scramble to accommodate.
jokes.explanation: The comedic value here is in the tonal whiplash — Moira's theatrical faucet complaint colliding with genuine emotional breakdown. The show balances this well, allowing both the comedy and the pathos to coexist. The Roses' fumbling response ('Oh boy, trouble with boys no doubt') is the real joke.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A drip? A *drip*? Do you understand what that means? It's not just water, it's the slow, methodical erosion of my will to live. Each drop is like a tiny hammer striking my very soul.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, it's a leaky faucet.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: A leaky faucet is what they used in medieval torture chambers, David. I'm calling a plumber. Actually, I'm calling a therapist. For the faucet.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Oh, boy troubles, no doubt. We don't want to intrude, dear.'
jokes.setup: Stevie is visibly breaking down with stress and anxiety about running the motel.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate diagnosis is 'boy troubles' — a wildly undersized explanation for an existential career crisis, delivered with the breezy confidence of a man who has solved the problem.
jokes.explanation: The gap between Johnny's casual read ('boy troubles') and the actual problem (existential business crisis) is the joke. His 'We don't want to intrude' while remaining very much in the room adds to the mild awkwardness.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I can't handle this. The stress is overwhelming. I don't know what to do.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh darling, it's quite simple really. You're just having commitment issues with your business.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Allow me to get you a pill. It's going to make you forget every last one of those irrational thoughts for at least two days!'
jokes.setup: Stevie is having a breakdown about not knowing how to run the motel.
jokes.punchline: Moira's solution is medication — offered warmly but with the specificity of 'for at least two days,' implying she knows the pharmacology from personal experience.
jokes.explanation: A strong Moira line — offering pills as a first resort is very on-brand, and 'for at least two days' suggests she's testing this empirically on herself. The warmth with which it's delivered makes it funnier than if it were meant dismissively.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Have you considered pharmaceutical amnesia? A little Ambien, a glass of pinot grigio, and you'll wake up tomorrow with absolutely no recollection of your business venture.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And if she needs any help at all, please feel free to come by our room.' / Johnny: 'Well, I'll be at work.' / Moira: 'But John will be there, implementing and strategizing.'
jokes.setup: Moira generously offers their room as a resource for Stevie.
jokes.punchline: Johnny clarifies he'll be at work, correcting Moira's implicit claim that he sits around all day — which Moira then confirms with the deliberately vague 'implementing and strategizing.'
jokes.explanation: 'Implementing and strategizing' is funny because it describes nothing — it's the language of someone who knows the vocabulary of business without the content. But the joke requires the setup of Johnny's correction to land, making it collaborative.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: And that's why you need a solid business plan. Take your father, for instance—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: What? Why am I the cautionary tale?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Because you're a perfect example of what happens when you don't prepare.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I prepared. I prepared extensively.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, you lost everything.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... that's not fair.

jokes.text: Moira: '(French accent) Courage!'
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to comfort the sobbing Stevie.
jokes.punchline: Moira delivers 'Courage!' in a theatrical French accent — a single word of encouragement that is both grandiose and completely unhelpful, as Stevie continues to sob.
jokes.explanation: Maximum economy — one word, in a French accent, tells you everything about Moira's theatrical, performative approach to emotional support. The French accent is unexplained and therefore funnier. The fact that it doesn't help at all (Stevie keeps sobbing) is the real punchline. One of the episode's most quotable single-word moments.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, Stevie. I have just the thing. I have a Xanax that I was prescribed in 1987 for a commercial audition that never happened. It's bound to still have some kick to it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Moira, I don't think that's—

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The expiration date has long since passed, which I'm told only makes them stronger. You'll feel nothing for anywhere between four and seven years.

jokes.text: David: 'Okay, I'm gonna need you to step out of the car for 20 minutes, so that I can do my transcendental meditation before I fail this test.'
jokes.setup: David and Alexis are parked before the driving test.
jokes.punchline: 'Before I fail this test' — David's pre-emptive concession that he is going to fail, delivered as a logistical matter to be planned around.
jokes.explanation: The buried 'before I fail this test' is a great comedic detail — David's self-awareness about his anxiety has crossed into pre-emptive acceptance of failure, which he's organized around rather than trying to overcome. Strong character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Nobody cares about your driving test, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What does that mean? That's a very cryptic statement.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Trust me, people aren't thinking about you the way that you're thinking about you.'
jokes.setup: David is catastrophizing about his driving test and what the examiner will think.
jokes.punchline: Alexis delivers a genuine insight — but framed so specifically around David's self-obsession that the advice is both accurate and a mild insult.
jokes.explanation: A quote that functions both as genuine advice and as a pointed observation about David's narcissistic anxiety. The line has some real standalone quotability and is one of the more grounded moments of comedy in the episode, functioning as a character insight rather than a pure joke.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You just skate through life without any real responsibilities or consequences.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I don't skate, David. I walk.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I just bought these Gianvito Rossi heels for $1,200.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I don't skate through life David, I walk through life. In really nice shoes.'
jokes.setup: David accuses Alexis of skating through life while others have to work for things.
jokes.punchline: Alexis corrects the metaphor — not skating, walking — and then adds 'in really nice shoes,' which simultaneously confirms David's point while being completely unaware that it does so.
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. Alexis doesn't understand that she's proving David's point by correcting his metaphor — and the shoe detail makes it worse, not better. The structure is perfect: the correction builds expectation of a defense, the 'really nice shoes' subverts it by adding detail that deepens the indictment. It's entirely character-specific — this is an Alexis Rose sentence. Extremely quotable, instantly recognizable as the show's specific comic voice. Approaches the 8+ anchor level clearly.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I fixed the drip.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You put a plastic bag under it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It collects the water.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: That's not fixing it, that's just... delaying the inevitable.

jokes.text: The driving examiner approaches the car — David and Alexis freeze mid-argument.
jokes.setup: David and Alexis are escalating into a genuine argument about fairness and privilege.
jokes.punchline: The examiner's arrival cuts the argument dead — David has to instantly compose himself and perform normalcy for a stranger.
jokes.explanation: A solid structural beat — the social interruption of a private fight is reliably funny. The timing of Alexis's 'The driving man's coming!' is endearingly childlike. Works mainly as a transitional beat rather than a pure joke.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Look, these things always work themselves out. Something will come up.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: You could just ask David for help.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, no, no. That's not going to work.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Nobody cares.' / David: 'I got it!'
jokes.setup: Earlier, Alexis told David 'nobody cares' about the driving test and his anxiety.
jokes.punchline: As the examiner approaches and David panics, Alexis feeds him his own punchline — 'nobody cares' — as a last-minute coaching cue, and David accepts it: 'I got it.'
jokes.explanation: A warm callback that doubles as an emotional beat — despite their argument, Alexis gives David the one line that helps. The brevity is perfect. 'I got it.' is a good button.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, maybe the second or third best idea you've ever had.

jokes.text: Johnny reveals his fix for the dripping faucet: 'I put a sandwich bag over the faucet and secured it with an elastic.'
jokes.setup: Johnny announces he has fixed the drip in David and Alexis's bathroom and Stevie can 'check that off her list.'
jokes.punchline: The 'fix' is a sandwich bag secured with a rubber band — a child's solution presented with the confidence of a competent handyman.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's proud presentation of his MacGyver solution is very funny — the specificity of 'sandwich bag' and 'elastic' makes it feel real and pathetic simultaneously. His confident opener ('I fixed the drip') followed by the reveal is well-structured misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm telling you, Bob, this pulp mill is state-of-the-art. Have you seen the floors? Spotless! Not a speck of dust anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: Roland, it's a pulp mill.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I know what it is, but when was the last time you saw a pulp mill with floors this clean? It's a game-changer.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Oh okay, won't the drips... fill up the sandwich bag?' / Johnny: 'Well, like I said, it's temporary, uh, but it might buy you a little time.' / Stevie: 'A little.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has explained his sandwich bag faucet fix.
jokes.punchline: Stevie's gentle but devastating 'A little.' — agreeing with his assessment while clearly communicating she does not find this reassuring.
jokes.explanation: Stevie's one-word response is the best kind of straight-man comedy — she doesn't argue, she just agrees technically while being completely unimpressed. 'A little.' as a complete sentence carries enormous weight. Economy score is very high.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: Name?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: David Rose.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: Okay... and what brings you in today?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm here for my driver's license exam.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: Right. Well, let's get started then, um...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: David. I just told you my name.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: Yes, yes. Of course.

jokes.text: Stevie suggests charging full price to long-term guests. Johnny: 'And sometimes it's the second or third idea that's really the keeper.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is enthusiastically building up to what he implies will be a breakthrough business idea for the motel.
jokes.punchline: Stevie offers the completely obvious suggestion of charging full price, and Johnny pivots instantly to 'sometimes the second or third idea is the keeper' — a face-saving deflection that reveals her idea was more obvious than his buildup suggested.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's expert pivot from having no ideas to endorsing someone else's idea while maintaining the posture of a guru is a good recurring Johnny beat. The line itself is solid corporate-speak used for personal deflection.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. Are you even listening to me? I'm pouring my heart out here.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: I'm listening. I'm just... filling out forms.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, could you fill out forms and make eye contact? I'm very vulnerable right now.

dialogue.speaker: Examiner
dialogue.line: I'm looking at you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, you're looking *past* me. There's a difference. One is soul-crushing.

jokes.text: Roland arrives and announces he needs to 'check out the new pulp mill' because 'I hear it's breathtaking.' The room reacts as if this is the most boring possible information.
jokes.setup: Moira has been building a case for heritage funding of the motel using the old pulp mill as a pathos-laden analogy.
jokes.punchline: Roland's enthusiastic interest in the new pulp mill is completely tone-deaf to the meeting's purpose and undercuts Moira's rhetorical momentum entirely.
jokes.explanation: Roland's enthusiasm for a pulp mill is a reliable small-town comedy beat — his civic priorities are completely misaligned with Moira's agenda. Works but doesn't transcend.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So how did it go with the council? Did you get the funding?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, not exactly. But I've been thinking about it, and failure is actually just success that hasn't learned to present itself properly.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: In fact, I would argue that my rejection today is merely the universe's way of redirecting my considerable talents toward a more spiritually aligned endeavor.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: The funding was never the point, Johnny. The point was the journey of self-discovery that comes with public humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So you didn't get it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I did not get it.

jokes.text: Moira: 'We all know, or are, people who live in the motel.'
jokes.setup: Moira is making a civic case for the motel's importance to the community.
jokes.punchline: 'Or are' — Moira casually acknowledging mid-speech that she herself is someone who lives in a motel, undermining the dignity of her argument while doubling down on it.
jokes.explanation: Excellent economy — the 'or are' is an almost parenthetical self-inclusion that lands as the joke while Moira plows forward. The humor is in her awareness that this admission undermines her, and the fact that she makes it anyway without breaking stride. Very good line.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You can't trust her. Last year she told me she was going to a yoga retreat in Sedona, and instead she went to East Asian palaces.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It was ONE time, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It was multiple palaces, Alexis.

jokes.text: Bob: 'Oh, just writing uh, "visit new pulp mill."' — Bob is supposed to be taking notes at a council meeting.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to read Bob's note-taking as engagement with her motel funding proposal.
jokes.punchline: Bob's note has nothing to do with the proposal — he's just writing down Roland's pulp mill tip as a personal to-do item.
jokes.explanation: A good payoff to Roland's pulp mill digression — the joke is that Bob was so moved by Roland's recommendation that he recorded it as an action item during an official council meeting. The mundane specificity of the note is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I think we should go into business together.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Well, I would advise against that. Mr. Rose has no business experience, no financial acumen, no practical skills of any kind, no relevant education, no references, and a crippling lack of confidence in even the simplest tasks.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I think that's a bit of an exaggeration.

dialogue.speaker: Ray
dialogue.line: Is it?

jokes.text: Council member: 'I thought you were against giving town funds to heritage projects. When we wanted to restore the war memorial, you said we should avoid taking sides.'
jokes.setup: Moira is making an impassioned case for funding the motel as a heritage project
jokes.punchline: Moira opposed funding a war memorial on grounds of 'taking sides' — an absurd position that is now being used against her by the very logic she established
jokes.explanation: The war memorial line is genuinely funny — 'avoid taking sides' applied to honoring war dead is a spectacularly bad political position. It reveals Moira's council tenure has been characterized by self-serving inconsistency. The callback to her own past position works well.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I fixed it. The leak is completely sealed.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, why is scalding water coming out of the cold tap?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's... that's a separate issue.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: So you've essentially created a booby trap in our only bathroom.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'll fix it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: When? After you fix the other thing you fixed?

jokes.text: Bob: 'Yeah, Roland got me thinking. I hear the floors are so clean you can eat your lunch off them.' / Roland: 'I laid those floors.'
jokes.setup: The council meeting has completely derailed into discussion of the new pulp mill.
jokes.punchline: Roland's proud, simple claim 'I laid those floors' — delivered as a moment of personal honor amid the most banal possible context.
jokes.explanation: Roland's quiet pride at having laid those floors is a small, perfect small-town character beat. His entire subplot in this meeting — derailing a council debate with pulp mill enthusiasm — pays off with this sincere little moment of civic pride.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: The hot water is coming out wrong.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, have you tried turning it off?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Now they can't turn it off and someone's getting burned.

jokes.text: The driving examiner calls David 'Derek' and then 'Rose' before getting to 'David Rose.'
jokes.setup: David is anxiously waiting in the car for the examiner.
jokes.punchline: The examiner can't get David's name right — 'Derek... Rose, David?' — a small, deflating moment of being unimportant to the one person whose judgment matters right now.
jokes.explanation: Quietly funny — the examiner's inability to remember David's name before he even gets in the car is a perfect small deflation of David's importance. Sets up the subsequent reveal that the examiner doesn't particularly care about David's test.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, the faucet is shooting water, it's on fire, and I can't turn it off.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: But if I can just get this temperature valve to work, everything will be fine.

jokes.text: David: 'I just didn't want you thinking you had gotten in the wrong car.'
jokes.setup: The examiner has confused David's name and David gently corrects him.
jokes.punchline: David's oddly formal, slightly absurd explanation for why the name matters — as if the examiner might otherwise abandon the car and seek the correct David.
jokes.explanation: A small but true David line — his anxiety expressing itself as over-explanatory politeness. The image of the examiner realizing he's in the wrong car and getting out mid-test is a funny implied visual.

jokes.text: David asks the examiner mid-driving-test: 'How old are you?' / 'Twenty-eight.' / 'And is this like, what you would like to do for a career?'
jokes.setup: David is in the middle of a driving test and has been warned that failure to signal will count against him.
jokes.punchline: David ignores all of this and begins conducting what is essentially a career interview with the examiner — processing his own anxiety by making it about the examiner's life choices.
jokes.explanation: A wonderful cringe-comedy beat — David's coping mechanism for anxiety is to redirect attention entirely, and he chooses to audit the examiner's life purpose during a driving test. The audacity of the career-satisfaction inquiry is very David and very funny. The examiner's confused 'Why?' is the perfect straight-man response.

jokes.text: David deduces: 'So you don't really care about this. Like, about me, in the car. I mean, you care in the sense that it's your job, but you don't-you don't care about this, do you?'
jokes.setup: The examiner has revealed he DJs on weekends and has done eight tests today.
jokes.punchline: David's meticulous unpacking of the difference between professional obligation-care and genuine-care — arriving at the conclusion that the examiner doesn't care, which is exactly what Alexis told him earlier.
jokes.explanation: This is a structural callback payoff — David is arriving, through his own investigation, at exactly the conclusion Alexis tried to give him for free. His over-analytical approach to receiving the same information is very character-specific. The distinction between 'caring as a job requirement' and 'caring' is genuinely well-observed.

jokes.text: Examiner: 'I've done like, eight of these today.' / David: 'Is that a problem?' / Examiner: 'No.'
jokes.setup: David has asked the examiner if he truly cares about David's test.
jokes.punchline: The examiner's 'I've done like, eight of these today' is a completely honest non-answer, and David's anxious 'Is that a problem?' followed by the flat 'No' is the perfect button.
jokes.explanation: The examiner's deadpan 'No' is the funniest word in this exchange — completely flat, not unkind, and perfectly unconcerned. The rhythm of the three-line exchange is tight.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Stop! I want to remember this moment.' / Johnny: 'What moment?'
jokes.setup: Johnny is getting enthusiastic about business ideas for the motel.
jokes.punchline: Moira dramatically freezes the moment as if it's historically significant — Johnny is confused because he doesn't see what she sees. The theater of Moira's response is completely disproportionate.
jokes.explanation: Moira treating ordinary moments with dramatic reverence is a reliable comedic mode. 'What moment?' from Johnny is the right confused response. Works as character comedy but not a standout.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Necessity drives invention, and far be it from me to rob you of your necessity!'
jokes.setup: Moira is justifying why she won't try to get council money for the motel — reframing her failure as intentional generosity.
jokes.punchline: She casts her inability to get funding as a gift — she is 'giving' Johnny the hardship as creative fuel. The spin is audacious and self-serving.
jokes.explanation: Moira repackaging her failures as strategic decisions is one of her signature moves, and 'rob you of your necessity' is an especially elegant formulation. The aphoristic quality ('necessity drives invention') gives it a faux-profundity that makes the self-serving reframe funnier.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'You didn't get the money, did you?' / Moira: 'Go, John! Before the passion dies again!'
jokes.setup: Moira has been elaborately justifying why no council money is actually better for Johnny.
jokes.punchline: Johnny cuts through all of it with 'You didn't get the money, did you?' — a flat, accurate read — and Moira's response is to completely ignore the question and redirect with theatrical urgency.
jokes.explanation: The best exchange of the motel subplot. Johnny's calm, accurate question ('You didn't get the money') followed by Moira's complete non-answer redirection ('Go, John!') is a perfect marriage of his groundedness and her theatrical evasion. 'Before the passion dies again!' is an especially good button — implying she's seen this moment of Johnny-enthusiasm before and knows it's perishable.

jokes.text: David and Alexis drive home; David confirms he passed, then describes the examiner: 'Slightly distracted. Transitioning to a career as a DJ, but apparently none of the venues around here could handle his sound.'
jokes.setup: We learned during the test that the examiner DJs on weekends.
jokes.punchline: David has apparently had a full conversation with the examiner about his DJ career — and reports it as ordinary contextual information, 'his sound' being the specific detail that makes it.
jokes.explanation: A satisfying callback — we now know David spent his driving test getting the examiner's full career story. 'None of the venues around here could handle his sound' is a delightful specific detail that implies both the examiner's grandiosity and the town's limitations.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'See? I told you. He didn't care.' / David: 'You never trust me!' / David: '...Yes, I do.' / Alexis: 'No, you don't!' — The circular argument that immediately follows his test success.
jokes.setup: David has just passed his driving test, which confirms Alexis's advice.
jokes.punchline: Rather than accepting the validation graciously, they immediately pivot to a new argument about whether David trusts Alexis — demonstrating that the sibling dynamic is inescapable.
jokes.explanation: The joke is structural — the moment of triumph is immediately replaced by a new grievance, which is true to the sibling relationship. Less a joke and more a character beat that sets up the episode's emotional resolution.

jokes.text: David: 'Not Mom and Dad. Me!' — after describing organizing temporary passports and colored contact lenses for Alexis
jokes.setup: David is revealing that he was the one managing Alexis's international crises
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'colored contact lenses' — necessary for Alexis's escapes, presumably for disguise — is a darkly funny detail, and David's emphatic 'Me!' completes the unexpected reversal of who was actually the responsible sibling
jokes.explanation: The 'colored contact lenses' detail is brilliantly specific — it implies covert identity changes as a regular part of Alexis's life, and the fact that David was ordering them is absurdly funny. The emotional reversal (David as the responsible one) is both a laugh and a genuine character revelation. Rewatch bonus because knowing the siblings' relationship makes this hit harder.

jokes.text: David: 'I was the one at home, not having fun because I was... constantly worried which East Asian palace Alexis was being held hostage in this week. Not Mom and Dad. Me!'
jokes.setup: David is explaining his anxiety about Alexis's disappearances as a child/teen.
jokes.punchline: 'Which East Asian palace Alexis was being held hostage in this week' — the absurdly specific nature of David's recurring worry, the word 'this week' implying it happened regularly, and the understated 'Not Mom and Dad. Me!'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines and its emotional heart. 'Which East Asian palace Alexis was being held hostage in this week' is extraordinary — the specificity ('East Asian'), the variety implied by 'which,' the normalization implied by 'this week.' It's both a joke about absurd Rose family privilege and a genuine window into David's childhood anxiety. The 'Not Mom and Dad. Me!' button is devastating in its simplicity. High marks across the board — original, character-specific, earns its dark turn, economical.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Well, you didn't have to worry about me.' / David: 'Well, I did.' — Beat. The car is quiet.
jokes.setup: David has revealed he spent years worrying about Alexis's safety.
jokes.punchline: The silence after 'Well, I did.' — a moment where the comedy has entirely converted into genuine emotion, and the joke is that this is happening in a car ride home from a driving test.
jokes.explanation: This beat is primarily emotional but functions as comedy through contrast — the mundane setting (post-driving-test car ride) for an unexpectedly tender revelation. The economy is exceptional: 'Well, I did.' Three words close a scene. The silence that follows is the show's warmth in action. High rewatch value as an earned emotional payoff.

jokes.text: Ray: 'I have my camera in the car' — responding to Stevie's casual mention of potentially selling the motel with immediate readiness to begin photographing it.
jokes.setup: Stevie mentions she's 'weighing all options' regarding the motel.
jokes.punchline: Ray has his camera in the car — he came prepared to list the motel immediately, revealing he was already treating this as a done deal.
jokes.explanation: Ray's preparedness is a good real estate agent joke — the camera in the car implies he assumed the listing before Stevie said anything. Efficient character comedy.

jokes.text: Ray: 'Either way, in reality, it's always best to tell the client what they want to hear.'
jokes.setup: Ray is saying there's never been a better time to sell a motel; Johnny is saying it's a terrible time.
jokes.punchline: Ray admits out loud, in front of both parties, that he just tells clients whatever they want to hear — an astonishing admission of professional dishonesty delivered as a business philosophy.
jokes.explanation: Ray's cheerful admission of professional manipulation in a room with his client is a great character moment — he has no self-awareness that this is damning. Delivered as wisdom rather than confession, which is the joke.

jokes.text: Ray: 'Stevie, as your agent, I would advise against partnering with somebody who has no capital, no contacts, no industry experience.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has offered to partner with Stevie to run the motel.
jokes.punchline: Ray, the agent who just admitted he tells clients what they want to hear, now offers candid (and accurate) advice against partnering with Johnny — choosing honesty at exactly the wrong moment.
jokes.explanation: Ironic callback — Ray uses his professional role to offer genuine (unflattering) advice immediately after admitting he just tells people what they want to hear. The hypocrisy is accidental and therefore funny. Good structure.

jokes.text: Ray: 'Oh well, this is all very exciting. However, if any of this falls through for any reason, perhaps one of the ones I mentioned, then... do give me a call.' / [Passes business card to Johnny]
jokes.setup: Johnny and Stevie have decided to partner and not sell the motel.
jokes.punchline: Ray gracefully accepts defeat while immediately planting a foot in the door — 'one of the ones I mentioned' referring to his list of Johnny's deficiencies, now repurposed as the terms of a future call.
jokes.explanation: Ray's exit move — turning his own criticism into future leverage — is a good piece of character comedy. The business card handed to Johnny ('Yeah. Okay.') is a nice button.

jokes.text: David: 'Yeah I just feel like the whole session was rushed. Like, there was no back lighting, or emotional direction.'
jokes.setup: David has just gotten his driver's license photo taken.
jokes.punchline: David critiques his driver's license photo as if it were a professional photoshoot — complaining about 'back lighting' and 'emotional direction' for a DMV document.
jokes.explanation: Very David — treating a DMV photo as a missed creative opportunity requiring 'emotional direction.' The gap between the context (bureaucratic ID photo) and David's expectations (bespoke portrait session) is the joke. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Nobody likes their driver's license photo, David.' / David: 'Okay yeah, I was just hoping I would get some options.'
jokes.setup: David is complaining about his license photo having no 'emotional direction.'
jokes.punchline: 'I was just hoping I would get some options' — David expected the DMV to provide multiple shots from which to select.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'no back lighting' to 'I just wanted options' is a great build — David's complaint system keeps revealing new depths of entitlement. 'Options' at the DMV is a perfect quotable moment because it's both absurd and exactly true to David's worldview.

jokes.text: Moira is revealed to have been in the bathroom the entire time the family was discussing the license photo — she has been fixing the sink.
jokes.setup: The family has been having a conversation outside the bathroom while everyone assumed the sink 'doesn't fix itself.'
jokes.punchline: Moira emerges from having apparently been in there the whole time — which retroactively makes her mysterious absence during the conversation funny, and raises questions about why she was fixing a sink.
jokes.explanation: Wait — on re-reading, it's Johnny who was in the bathroom fixing it ('I'm gonna go... [thumps]'). Johnny's reveal that he's been in there 'the whole time' is the joke — he fixed the sink while everyone was talking outside. Let me re-attribute. The comedy is that Johnny has been silently in the bathroom the entire time and nobody noticed.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Have you been in there the whole time?!' — Johnny has apparently been in the bathroom fixing the faucet while the family gathered in the room.
jokes.setup: The family has been having conversations outside the bathroom, unaware anyone was in there.
jokes.punchline: Johnny emerges to reveal he's been in the bathroom the whole time — 'The sink doesn't fix itself' — and invites the family to 'check it out' with the pride of a craftsman.
jokes.explanation: A good escalation of the plumbing subplot — Johnny has been in the bathroom this whole time, which retroactively adds a silent comedy layer to the preceding scenes. His pride in unveiling the repair sets up the disastrous hot water reveal.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Okay kids, give it a spin.' — proudly presenting his faucet repair to the family.
jokes.setup: Johnny has fixed the faucet and invites the family to test it, with the confidence of a professional.
jokes.punchline: What follows is a cascade of disaster — hot water from the cold tap, burning, steam, nobody can turn it off.
jokes.explanation: The setup line here does all the work — 'give it a spin' with paternal pride earns the catastrophe that follows. Works as a structural joke whose punchline is the entire subsequent sequence.

jokes.text: David: 'You changed it so that the hot water comes out of the cold water tap?'
jokes.setup: Johnny has 'fixed' the faucet and the family is testing it.
jokes.punchline: The hot and cold taps have been reversed — Johnny's fix has made the problem worse in a new and unexpected way.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Johnny's plumbing incompetence — from sandwich bag to reversed taps — is well-paced across the episode. The confusion in David's question captures exactly the reaction a viewer has: this is a new and specific kind of wrong.

jokes.text: The faucet chaos escalates: Moira reports steam, nobody can turn it off, and someone burns their hand — culminating in Johnny: 'See? No drip.'
jokes.setup: Johnny has fixed the dripping faucet but reversed the hot and cold taps.
jokes.punchline: 'See? No drip.' — Johnny's triumphant conclusion as the family recoils from scalding water and steam. He has technically fixed the original problem.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best-structured joke and its biggest laugh. 'See? No drip.' is structurally perfect — Johnny is technically correct, which makes it impossible to argue with and therefore funnier. The entire plumbing subplot has been building to this single line: the sandwich bag, the elastic, Stevie's 'a little,' Johnny's partnership offer to handle plumbing, and now this triumph. The economy is exceptional — three words close an entire B-plot. The chaos of steam and burning hands makes the understatement land harder. This is a genuine 8+ moment, comparable in structure and payoff to the best anchor examples.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I'm gonna go.' — immediately leaving the bathroom after his 'See? No drip.' triumph as Moira shouts 'Ow!'
jokes.setup: The faucet is spraying scalding water and nobody can turn it off.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's casual exit — 'I'm gonna go' — as if his work here is done, leaving the family to deal with the scalding consequences of his repair.
jokes.explanation: A perfect button. 'I'm gonna go' has the energy of someone who has checked their task off a list and has no further business here. The contrast between the chaos (steam, burns, shouting) and Johnny's serene departure is the joke. Earns its score through timing and character voice.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira worry about the future of the motel and David panics at the thought of retaking his driving test.
insight: Character escalation drives 42 jokes in 42 minutes—Moira's driving test audition peaks at 83.1.
